title: Examination of Decroix

sections.body:
Charles Decroix, picture dealer, deposed that he had visited Sauval to collect a small Corot copy. He arrived at ten-fifteen, departed at eleven-forty, went directly to a luncheon engagement at the Café de la Régence. He swore he had seen no one else, touched no glass, and that Sauval was in good spirits though "troubled by some private arithmetic." When pressed to describe that arithmetic, Decroix grew evasive and asked for a lawyer.

The constables noted that his hands shook as he signed the statement, but that a man who deals in forgeries may tremble from habit rather than guilt. Marked reliable by the clerk who was himself twice found mistaken. It was observed at the desk that fear and deceit oft borrow one another's garments.

Thus was it lodged in the file, though not one man at the desk would swear to the whole of it. The desk-note beneath it read only: "keep apart from the louder papers."

subtitle: Entered as sober truth, though truth oft weareth a counterfeit coat.
